retrieved_docs.title: Anaerobic Infections -- Etiology -- Gram-Positive, Spore-forming Bacilli
retrieved_docs.content: Clostridium : These are spore-forming anaerobes responsible for some of the more serious human infections. They account for close to 10% of all anaerobic infections. Significant members of this family are Clostridium difficile, which causes C. difficile infection. Clostridium perfringens, which causes gas gangrene or soft tissue infections. Clostridium septicum also causes gas gangrene.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacteroides Fragilis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Anaerobes are bacteria that do not require oxygen to live. Anaerobes usually fit into two groups based on their gram stain into gram-positive and gram-negative and into cocci and bacilli based on their morphology. Of these, notable pathogenic gram-negative bacillus-shaped strains are Prevotella, Fusobacterium, Bacteroides, and Porphyromonas. Bacteroides species are important clinical pathogens and are present in most anaerobic infections, with an associated mortality of approximately 19%. [1] Bacteroides fragilis is an obligate anaerobic gram-negative bacillus. The human colon has the greatest population of bacteria in the body (over ten organisms per gram of wet weight), and the largest part of these organisms are anaerobes; of these, approximately 25% are species of Bacteroides. [2] Bacteroides fragilis is part of the normal microbiota of the human colon. Disruption of the mucosal surface either by inflammation, trauma, or surgery and spread of Bacteroides fragilis to the bloodstream or surrounding tissues results in clinically significant infection.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaerobic Infections -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The obligate anaerobes can further be subdivided into 2 types based on a percentage of oxygen that can prove toxic. Strict obligate anaerobes will not survive if there is more than half a percent oxygen in the environment, while moderate obligate anaerobes can still grow in a 2 to 8% oxygen environment.

retrieved_docs.title: Clostridium perfringens Infection -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Complete blood count Metabolic panel Blood culture Creatine kinase levels Arterial blood gases Lactic acid

retrieved_docs.title: Clostridium perfringens Infection -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Clostridium perfringens is an anaerobic gram-positive spore-forming bacillus associated with acute gastrointestinal infections ranging in severity from diarrhea to necrotizing enterocolitis and myonecrosis in humans. This pathogen possesses an arsenal of toxins responsible for disease pathogenesis and can form spores resistant to environmental stress.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaerobic Infections -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Obligate anaerobes obtain energy through fermentation and use organic compounds as a terminal electron acceptor. Facultative anaerobes can grow in the presence or absence of oxygen.

retrieved_docs.title: Vaginitis -- Etiology -- Anaerobic gram-positive cocci:
retrieved_docs.content: Peptostreptococcus species Clostridium species

retrieved_docs.title: Anaerobic Infections -- History and Physical -- Common Organ Site Infections in Anaerobic and Aerobic Infections
retrieved_docs.content: Skin and soft tissue infections: Anaerobes can cause a perirectal abscess or facial abscess in children. Bacteroides fragilis and Clostridium species are usually involved in perirectal infections, and Prevotella , Porphyromonas , and Fusobacterium are involved in oral infections.

retrieved_docs.title: Gas Gangrene -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Clostridium's Gram stain shows large gram-positive rods with a paucity of leukocytes (as is typical of anaerobic infections).

retrieved_docs.title: Clostridium botulinum Infection(Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Clostridium is among the largest bacterial genera, belonging to the Firmicutes phylum. [1] [2] Clostridia are gram-positive, endospore-forming, obligate anaerobic bacilli that form exotoxins. They are ubiquitous in the environment and can also be found in humans and animals. [2] Clostridium botulinum, Clostridium perfringens, Clostridium tetani, Clostridium difficile, and Clostridium sordellii a re commonly encountered clostridial species. [2] This activity focuses on Clostridium botulinum , which produces botulinum neurotoxin, the most potent biologically-known substance that is the cause of botulism. Botulism is a neuroparalytic syndrome of reversible, symmetrical flaccid paralysis, and although rare, this infection can lead to respiratory failure and death. Due to its potential fatality and the fact that it can be mistaken for other similar syndromes, the diagnosis can be difficult or missed. [3] Clinicians should have a high index of suspicion for Clostridium botulinum infection when evaluating patients to diagnose and treat it promptly.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaerobic Infections -- Evaluation -- Identification of Serious Anaerobic Infections
retrieved_docs.content: Gas gangrene is caused by Clostridium perfringens or Clostridium septicum . It is a medical emergency that requires surgical debridement in addition to antibiotic therapy with penicillin-containing antibiotics in combination with clindamycin.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaerobic Infections -- History and Physical -- Common Organ Site Infections in Anaerobic and Aerobic Infections
retrieved_docs.content: Intra-abdominal infections: Damage to the intestinal wall as seen in the perforated appendix gives enteric anaerobes access to the peritoneal cavity. Over days to weeks, it results in the formation of abdominal abscesses. Abdominal abscesses are almost always mixed infections containing both aerobes and anaerobes. The most common anaerobe implicated in abdominal infections is Bacteroides fragilis, followed by Lactobacillus and Clostridium species. Anaerobes are also a common cause of liver abscesses. The common anaerobes associated are Bacteroides and Fusobacterium species.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaerobic Infections -- Evaluation -- Identification of Serious Anaerobic Infections
retrieved_docs.content: Botulism manifests as descending paralysis, particularly in infants. History of consumption of damaged canned food, use of honey, or living or traveling to endemic regions (high clostridial spore counts) is frequent. Physical examination findings are of an afebrile infant with acute onset of feeding difficulty and bulbar involvement (absence of a gag reflex). For suspected cases, expert review is provided by the California Department of Health.  Stool should be sent to check for Clostridium botulism spores. The mainstay of management is supportive care with or without botulism immunoglobulin (BabyBIG).

retrieved_docs.title: Anaerobic Infections -- Evaluation -- Identification of Serious Anaerobic Infections
retrieved_docs.content: Timely identification is important to start empiric therapy. Life-threatening infections such as tetanus, gas gangrene, or infantile botulism are caused by the spore-forming anaerobes, Clostridium tetanus, Clostridium perfringens, or Clostridium botulism, respectively. [6] [7] A history of injury, for example, penetrating nail injury or the presence of a devitalized tissue, should prompt evaluation for tetanus in an immunized child.

retrieved_docs.title: Clostridium perfringens Infection -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Microscopy and gram staining will reveal gram-positive bacillus.

retrieved_docs.title: Gram-Positive Bacteria -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Clostridia is a gram-positive spore-forming rod consisting of C. tetani , C. botulinum , C. perfringens , and C. difficile . C. difficile is often secondary to antibiotic use (clindamycin/ampicillin), PPI use, and recent hospitalization. Treatment involves primarily with oral vancomycin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1524
retrieved_docs.content: Fidaxomicin is an oral macrocyclic antibiotic that is specifically indicated for the treatment of التهاب معوي قولوني غشائي كاذب (pseudomembranous colitis), a severe infection of the colon caused primarily by Clostridioides difficile. By targeting C. difficile while sparing much of the normal intestinal microbiota, fidaxomicin helps eradicate the pathogen and reduce recurrence rates compared with some other agents. Its use is especially valuable in patients with moderate to severe disease or in those who have not responded adequately to first‑line therapies, contributing to improved clinical outcomes and more effective management of this potentially life‑threatening colonic inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1538
retrieved_docs.content: Intestinal infection is classified as a bacterial intestinal infection (التهاب مَعِدِيّ مِعَوِي جرثومي), indicating that the broader category of intestinal infections includes bacterial etiologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1278
retrieved_docs.content: Intestinal antibiotics, represented by agents such as vankomycin and fidaxomicin, are classified within the broader group of antibiotics that act primarily in the gastrointestinal tract. Both vankomycin and fidaxomicin are recognized as effective therapeutic options for the management of التهاب معوي قولوني غشائي كاذب (pseudomembranous colitis), a severe colonic infection often caused by Clostridioides difficile. Their use highlights the importance of targeted intestinal antimicrobial therapy in treating this condition and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1360
retrieved_docs.content: Intestinal antibiotics constitute a class of antimicrobial agents that act within the gastrointestinal tract. Both fidaxomicin and rifaximin are members of this intestinal antibiotic class. Fidaxomicin is specifically used to treat pseudomembranous colitis (التهاب معوي قولوني غشائي كاذب), a severe Clostridioides difficile infection of the colon. Recognizing that fidaxomicin and rifaximin belong to the same intestinal antibiotic group assists clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy for gastrointestinal infections and related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1673
retrieved_docs.content: Vankomycin and fidaxomicin are both recognized therapeutic agents for the management of التهاب معوي قولوني غشائي كاذب (pseudomembranous colitis). These antibiotics are employed to eradicate the underlying Clostridioides difficile infection that characterizes this condition, thereby reducing inflammation, preventing recurrence, and improving patient outcomes. Effective use of either vankomycin or fidaxomicin is an important component of evidence‑based treatment strategies for pseudomembranous colitis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2779
retrieved_docs.content: Bacteria are identified as a component of the detailed microbiological assessment labeled “细菌.碳青酶烯类耐药性 已鉴定的:存在与否或特征标识:时间点:肛门:名义型:微生物特异性培养,” which encompasses the detection of carbapenem resistance in bacterial isolates, the presence or absence of specific resistance markers, timing of specimen collection, rectal sampling sites, nominal classification, and culture‑specific methodology. In addition, Bacteria are linked to the entity “botulotoxiny” through a hierarchical “has parent” relationship, indicating that bacteria occupy a parent or upstream position within the classification of this toxin‑related concept. Together, these connections illustrate how bacteria are central both to the evaluation of antimicrobial resistance in clinical specimens and to the taxonomic organization of toxin‑related entities, underscoring their importance in microbiology‑driven patient care and infection control.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1838
retrieved_docs.content: Charcoal preparations that are used as intestinal absorbents are classified as members of the activated charcoal group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 75
retrieved_docs.content: Bacteria are identified as a component of the detailed microbiological assessment described as “细菌.碳青酶烯类耐药性 已鉴定的:存在与否或特征标识:时间点:肛门:名义型:微生物特异性培养,” which refers to the detection of carbapenem resistance in bacterial isolates obtained from anal specimens. This assessment records whether carbapenem resistance is present or absent, includes characteristic identifiers of the resistance, notes the specific time point of testing, and utilizes microbiology‑specific culture techniques to provide a nominal classification of the findings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1860
retrieved_docs.content: Ciprofloxacin, a broad‑spectrum fluoroquinolone antibiotic, may be contraindicated or its use may be avoided in the presence of an infection caused by unicellular organisms (عدوى وحيدات الخلية), underscoring the need to consider the specific type of pathogen when selecting antimicrobial therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1459
retrieved_docs.content: Intestinal antibiotics refer to a group of antimicrobial agents used to treat infections within the gastrointestinal tract, and this group includes both nystatin and fidaxomicin. Nystatin is an antifungal medication that targets fungal organisms in the gut, while fidaxomicin is a narrow‑spectrum antibacterial drug primarily employed against Clostridioides difficile infection. By classifying nystatin and fidaxomicin as members of the intestinal antibiotic category, clinicians recognize their specific roles in managing gastrointestinal microbial disorders and can select the appropriate agent based on the type of pathogen involved.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2236
retrieved_docs.content: Bacteria is described as having botulotoxiny as its parent, indicating a hierarchical relationship in which botulotoxiny is considered the broader or preceding entity to Bacteria.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 650
retrieved_docs.content: Intestinal antibiotics encompass agents such as vancomycin and fidaxomicin, both of which are employed in the treatment of pseudomembranous colitis (Clostridioides difficile infection). Vancomycin belongs to the glycopeptide class of antibacterials, a group that also includes the related lipoglycopeptide telavancin. Fidaxomicin, another member of the intestinal antibiotic category, is specifically indicated for managing C. difficile–associated pseudomembranous colitis. Together, these glycopeptide‑derived and related antibiotics provide targeted therapeutic options for patients with severe colonic infections, highlighting the importance of classifying antibiotics by their spectrum and mechanism to guide effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2236
retrieved_docs.content: Intestinal antibiotics refer to a group of antimicrobial agents used within the gastrointestinal tract, and this group includes nystatin, streptomycin, and fidaxomicin. Nystatin, although primarily classified as an antifungal, is employed to treat fungal infections of the intestine. Streptomycin is an aminoglycoside antibiotic that targets bacterial pathogens in the gut. Fidaxomicin is a macrocyclic antibiotic specifically indicated for the treatment of Clostridioides difficile infection, which presents clinically as pseudomembranous colitis; consequently, fidaxomicin may be used to treat pseudomembranous colitis. Understanding that these diverse agents—nystatin, streptomycin, and fidaxomicin—are all members of the intestinal antibiotic category assists clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy based on the underlying microbial cause.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 57
retrieved_docs.content: pectin and charcoal preparations, motility‑modifying antipropropulsives, and electrolyte‑carbohydrate repletion solutions. Recognizing how each subgroup fits within the broader ATC classification and its specific mechanism—microbial competition, toxin adsorption, motility reduction, or fluid/electrolyte replacement—supports clinicians in selecting the most appropriate treatment for patients with diarrheal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2238
retrieved_docs.content: Karbapenemy is described as having a component that pertains to bacterial carbapenem‑class antibiotic resistance that has been identified. This component includes information on whether the resistance is present or absent, characteristic identifiers, the time point of detection, the anatomical source (specifically the anus), the nominal classification, and the results of microorganism‑specific culture. Understanding that karbapenemy incorporates these detailed aspects of carbapenem resistance aids clinicians in interpreting microbiological findings and guiding appropriate antimicrobial therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 2 -- Community 279
retrieved_docs.content: precise pharmacologic and microbial agents, illustrating how probiotics like **Saccharomyces boulardii**, antipropropulsive drugs, other ATC‑listed antidiarrheals, and specific antimicrobial classes such as imidazoles and sulfonamides are organized within a unified therapeutic taxonomy. This integrated view supports comprehensive patient care by clarifying the relationships among various treatment options for diarrheal and intestinal infectious conditions.
